speaker: interviewer
text: When he gives me the one-minute signal, I usually don't take the full minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when-- until-- Mira, when we left off at the last tape, you were in Vilna in school. And you were telling me about how when your father would visit, you would cry when he had to leave. Continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why did I cry? Because I was very close to him. Because it meant-- it felt good. By saying good, I mean it felt safe. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It was pleasant to be with him. I remember at home when we used to sit together. And he would read with me Tanakh in Hebrew-- explain to me, tell me, what is it all about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember when he would explain to me math, like, for instance, algebra. He did not know algebra. But he would explain it his own way, using his logic. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that-- it really felt good. It felt something that I have not only a father sitting next to me, but a close, close friend. So I, I long for him. I longed for him for a long time. I longed for my mother, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who gave me so much, who would listen to me. At times, I would come home-- worry about things. She would always take her time, sit down near me and listen to me. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I missed them so much. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I missed them because I, I knew that they'll never, never be with me again.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went back from Vilna to your hometown, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] do you remember the, the first memory of, of the change in, in the political climate?

speaker: interviewee
text: I always longed to go back to my town. And the time when I would go back was during vacation-- vacation time, summertime. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But Wilno, when we compare Wilno to my own town, to Zhetel, it was such a huge-- it seemed to be so big. And when I came back to everything looked so small. The houses, the homes were small. There-- no transportation exactly. Because we had near Wilno a little town, Nowojelnia, N-O-V-O-E-L-N-I-A. That's the way you spelled it-- where they had a train station. So I would go from Zhetel to Nowojelnia by bus or-- summertime, wintertime by sleigh, and then catch the train in Nowojelnia, and go by train to Wilno. That was another experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] That was another experience. It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would go out into the world. To go to Wilno-- Wilno was something. Go by train-- it would take me half a day to go by train from Zhetel to Wilno. And there I would come, and-- as I said before-- stay with my uncle. But later on, I got my own little room. And I moved. But I was very close to my uncle. He would come and see me almost every day because where I used to live was very close near his house. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Wilno was something special to me. I thought that I'll never leave Wilno. I thought that I will attend a school where they'll teach me to become a teacher. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But the war interrupted us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your first memory of, of the war, of the change?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first memory is when Germany concluded the nonaggression pact with Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1939. And at that point, they tore apart Poland. The eastern part was taken away by the Russians. And the western part was taken away by the Germans. And only a narrow strip was left for Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember that we questioned this pact. We wondered, will Germany ever attack Russia? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in spite of this pact, in 1941, Germany attacked Russia. I want you to tell me a little bit about the-- what-- how life changed for you after this.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the Russians take over-- took over-- took over our areas, right? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] First of all, they changed the schools. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] first of all, I couldn't go anymore back to Wilno.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that point, I left for Nowogrodek, N-O-V-O-G-R-D-E-K. I left for Nowogrodek. And I attended [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a Polish school-- a Polish school, where all the subjects were taught in Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then, when the Russians took over our towns, they changed the Polish schools into all Russian schools, [NON-ENGLISH]. That means that you studied at the school for 10 years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's where I learned Russian. So it was a change.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It affected me in a way that I could not study Polish anymore. And all the subjects were taught in Russian. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I lived in a internat, they call it. That means a place where all the students were placed. And the food wasn't very good, but we survived. We survived. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then-- and then, when Germany attacked our areas, I went back to Zhetel. And the Germans occupied our towns.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the-- did the Germans occupy Nowogrodek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The-- Germany occupied Nowogrodek, Zdzieciol, all the towns.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember when, when the Germans came into Nowogrodek?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans, when they came in, first, I mean, it was the Wehrmacht. That means that they treated us fairly. It wasn't bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we felt, it's not that bad. What are they going to do? They will take away from us everything, but they're not going to kill us. Who were--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's--

speaker: interviewer
text: --the Wehrmacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what-- that's what we felt in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the Wehrmacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those were the civilians who came in, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] soldiers-- German soldiers and civilians. And they started to organize our town. That's when they formed the ghetto. The Germans' goal was to eliminate the Jews in particular, and the minorities in general. And in order to achieve this goal, they had two tasks. One was to eliminate all the intellectuals-- the teachers, then the students, the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] all, all the elements. In other words, all the elements who were capable to resist and to organize, they eliminated them. And then they formed ghettos. The ghetto is a certain part of town where all the Jews from all over town they were pushed into this particular area.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want you to tell me-- go back for a minute to eliminating the intellectuals. This phrase-- what did that mean? What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: That means that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they had a list of all the intellectuals in our town. Where did they get the list? They got it from the local authorities. The local authorities who lived in our town, they knew all the Jews who lived in, in, in Zhetel. They made a list. A matter of fact, that my father was also on the list. And they came-- I remember they came to my house, and they asked for my father. And my father was a weak person. He was-- he had problems with his, his-- I guess he had, like, you would say today, emphysema, or something like that, that he had difficulty-- he developed in this-- in his later years an allergy. And he was known in town as he suffered from it. So then they came and they asked for him, we came out with my mother and with my sister and brother. And we said that he's sick. And they left. And we saved my father for a little longer. They took all the people. They gathered them. They put them into trucks. And they were driven away. And they were never seen again. And then they had the nerve to come back to our town and to ask for, for some warm clothes for our loved ones. The loved ones were gone a long time ago. They took them to a special place and they killed them there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness any of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The peasants. The peasants came to our town, and they told us about. And that was the time when all the intellectuals-- all the teachers, all the-- all the students-- they were taken away. And there was one woman, she was a registered nurse. So she was taken with them. And they were killed. We never saw them again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you feel about this? What were the rest of the people--

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --doing at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were scared. We were scared. We did not know, at that point, what happened to them. They came and they asked for some warm clothes. So, so we brought them everything what we had. But, in fact, they were-- they were gone. The peasants told us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before they came back for the clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: The peasants told us later that they saw them-- they saw them being killed. That was horrible. It was just a terrible, terrible, terrible thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this before the ghetto was formed?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was before the ghetto was formed. That was before the ghetto was formed. Then they came out, and they ask us to bring all our jewelry-- fur, jewelry, whatever we had. Everybody took everything, whatever they had, and they brought it. The Germans took it. Then they started to form the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: [SIGHS] What's your first--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ghetto life was not easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your first memory of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was difficult. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They would send us out early in the morning to do different jobs. To take us-- first of all, we would work in the ghetto at different areas, at different places for the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe first the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was like a round circle-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a round circle. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] here, I'll inject a personal experience. We were a family of six people. And we lived in four rooms. And another four families were pushed in-- into our living quarters. About 30, 35 people-- excuse me-- lived in our-- in our place. It wasn't easy. It was not pleasant at all. But we had no choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some strangers.

speaker: interviewer
text: People you didn't know?

speaker: interviewee
text: People we didn't know. Can you imagine 35 people living in four rooms?

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe that for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe a typical--

speaker: interviewee
text: --very--

speaker: interviewer
text: --day.

speaker: interviewee
text: --difficult. It was very difficult. We used to sleep on the floor-- naturally, so many people. As far as cooking, everybody would, would try to, to prepare something in this little kitchen. It was horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe a typical day for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: A typical day-- we were sent out to work. Some were left-- elderly, children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The people who worked came late at night, returned tired, exhausted, depressed. We always had a few people always sitting and watching through the window to see what is happening if we are not surrounded. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And every time, when we had a false alarm, we would grab our backpacks that we had prepared. But when it really happened, we left everything behind, and we ran to our hiding places. When we were in the ghetto, rumors started to spread around about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jews being killed in the neighborhood towns. We started to build hiding places. In our house, we had two hiding places. One was a big place where we stored away food and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] medications. And another place was a little place that was a double wall in a chicken house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember when it happened in our town, when they surrounded us. We ran with my sister to the hiding place. We came to the large hiding place. And there was so many people-- children crying. We decided we'll take a risk and we'll go to the small hiding place-- the double wall. We were hiding in this place for two nights.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember that when we were in the hiding place, the Germans, they were chasing a chicken. Can you imagine how we felt when we were hearing them running around and they're looking for the chicken. And finally, they caught the chicken, and they left-- our relief. We were in this hiding place with another three men, but we never saw them. Because it was dark in the hiding place. And when we came out from the hiding place, it was also dark. Because we had to leave the ghetto at night so nobody will see us and nobody will hear us. But I remember that I was so clumsy that I stepped on every wire. And it made so much noise. But we made it. We made it. We left the ghetto and escaped to the forest. First-- excuse me-- first, first, we escaped to the labor camp what I mentioned, Dworez, D-W-O-R-E-Z. And then from this labor camp, we left for the forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go outside the ghetto, I-- tell me the-- I want to go back a little bit. The day that they formed the ghetto, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you described for me before how they actually formed it. And tell me how-- what you remember of the day that they created the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, when they formed the ghetto, our house where we lived was part of the ghetto. So we did not have to move. So I don't know exactly how it happened, the way people moved to the ghetto, because I was in the ghetto. But I know one thing. When they came to the ghetto, they did not take anything with them. When they moved in-- into our quarters, living quarters, they just-- I guess they had the-- more the valuable things, whatever they had. But otherwise, they could not take anything with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the ghetto closed off?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was closed off. There was a fence all around. You could not get in. You couldn't get out. Only the people who were going to work in the morning, they would get out. The people who worked out-- on the outside of the ghetto. But the people who worked in the ghetto, they, they never saw the outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would they decide who stayed where?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a Judenrat. That means-- that means people were chosen to lead us. They, the Germans, demanded to give them a list of people. And the Judenrat had to form the list. It was very hard on them because sometimes they would ask for at least 40 people to, to be killed, to be taken away and never return. And the Judenrat had to make this list. So it was very hard of them-- for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: They--

speaker: interviewer
text: --pick the Jewish leaders? How did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: How did we pick the Jewish leaders? We-- they were appointed.

speaker: interviewer
text: By whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were appointed by the people-- by the people who lived in the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they were the leaders. They were the leaders. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everybody had to work. If you worked, you had a Schein, they call it. That means-- that means you had a document. And that helped you, in a way, to survive. Not always, not always, not always, but you had a better chance. Let's put it this way-- you had a better chance to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because you worked somewhere, especially people in our town who specialized in certain occupations. Like, for instance-- like, for instance, my uncle, he was a tailor. But he was a good tailor-- a good tailor. So he had a Schein for him and for his whole family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So when it-- and they had them-- shop, the workshop, out of the ghetto-- outside of the ghetto. So when it happened, when they had the action, when they surrounded our ghetto, and when they liquidate the ghetto, he took his whole family to the shop. And he was hiding there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, it didn't work out because the Germans found the shop, and they killed everybody there. But he felt that by having the Schein, he will be able to survive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] do you want me to describe you a little bit the action at the end when they liquidated the ghetto? I do, but if you want to tell us more about ghetto life-- how long you were there, and what it was like for you, individually?

speaker: interviewee
text: For me individually, I was so young. I worked-- OK, I worked-- I worked for the Russians. I worked for the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was afraid, in a way, to find work in the ghetto. When the Germans-- because I was afraid that somebody will tell them that I was working for, for the-- for the Soviets, for the Russians. What were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working in the [NON-ENGLISH]. That means-- that means-- that means financial department as, as an office worker. Not much, not much, because I was young, and I couldn't-- I didn't know it that well. But I was working-- employed. That's why, maybe that was just my imagination. Maybe it wouldn't even happen anything. But I wasn't exactly anxious to find a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old--

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- but people, people who were in the ghetto, they found little jobs. They found little jobs, like, like, in the office where the leaders used to work, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like, to work for the Germans, to clean, to wash, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like, people who had their occupations. I was a student. So I did not have my occupation. But people who had their occupations, like, for instance, a carpenter who could-- who could produce, who could, see, work for the Germans, who could make-- who could make boots for them-- shoes. They felt they had a better chance to survive. And they did have a better chance to survive. The first-- during the first selections, they took all the elderly people. They took the young ones. I remember they took away from us my grandma. And she did not-- she did not know German. But she was begging him. She said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I am a grandmother. But they just took her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you there when they took her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there with my mother and father and my grandmother. They tore away my grandmother from us. And, and we-- and we-- and she begged, and we begged. But they took her. And we were left, my mother, father, my brother and sister. That was the first action. The first action. And then we went back to our quarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took-- where were you when they took her?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we were-- they asked us to gather at a certain place in our town. And they told us that they're going to check our documents. But instead, they took the elderly. They took the young children. They took them away. And we were left. And we went back to our quarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they tell you when they took your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't tell us anything. They just tore her away from us and took her away. And that was the last time when I saw my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took her on a truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: They, they took them-- they took all the people. They took them to a certain place at the cemetery far away from our town. And they killed them there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: They had prepared-- they had prepared-- they had prepared holes where they just pushed them in-- shot them.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to break again to change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you know, I didn't talk--
